name: list-criteria
description: Numbered criteria list — each requirement is a row with rationale.
purpose: Use to enumerate the criteria a decision must meet, in priority order. Numbering signals weight; each row reads as a complete requirement.

criteria.description: One li per criterion. Author as `**Criterion name**` then a nested `- rationale` bullet (card-style nested format, not the forbidden inline `**Title.** body`).

skeleton:
<!-- _class: list-criteria -->

## What every decision must satisfy.

1. **First criterion**
   - Short rationale for why this matters.
2. **Second criterion**
   - Short rationale.
3. **Third criterion**
   - Short rationale.
4. **Fourth criterion**
   - Short rationale.
sample:
<!-- _class: list-criteria -->

## What every component manifest must satisfy.

1. **Stable name**
   - Kebab-case, matching the `_class` directive authors type when invoking the component.
2. **Function coordinate**
   - One of seven families per the four-layer model: anchor, statement, inventory, comparison, progression, evidence, imagery.
3. **Form coordinate**
   - One of eleven spatial shapes: bookend, divider, canvas, grid, stack, ledger, panel, matrix, scatter, timeline, split.
4. **Substance coordinate**
   - One of four plugin contracts: prose, structure, series, graph (or mixed for panel-form components).
5. **Skeleton plus sample**
   - Skeleton scaffolds blank slides for the new-slide CLI; sample demonstrates the component substantively for the gallery.

whenToUse.title: Criteria that must all be satisfied
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs to read each requirement as a complete gate, not a suggestion. The numbered ledger format signals 'these are the rules' rather than 'here are some options'.

whenToUse.title: Order encodes priority
whenToUse.body: The leading-zero counter (`01`, `02`, …) reads as rank. Put the load-bearing criterion first; the audience uses position as a weight.

whenToUse.title: Three to six rows
whenToUse.body: Below three the ledger feels under-furnished; above six the row gap closes and the audience loses scannability. Group adjacent criteria or split into two slides.

antiPatterns.title: Parallel options, not gates
antiPatterns.body: If the items are alternatives the audience is choosing between, use `cards-grid` or `verdict-grid`. list-criteria is for requirements all of which must hold.

antiPatterns.title: Rationale longer than two lines
antiPatterns.body: Each row is a one-sentence rationale. If a criterion needs a paragraph, lift it to `list-steps` or `split-brief` where the body has room to breathe.

antiPatterns.title: Missing criterion title
antiPatterns.body: The bold lead on each li is what makes the ledger scannable. A naked sentence per row reads as paragraph soup; the bold title is the structure.

related.name: list-steps
related.when: rows are procedural steps with longer body, not gating criteria

related.name: principles
related.when: tenets or values rather than gates a decision must clear

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: rows carry structured metadata alongside the name and description
